questions.to_find: An expression equivalent to toggling a binary variable x between 0 and 1.

understand.plain: The variable x holds either 0 or 1. The if-else flips (toggles) x: if x is 0 it becomes 1, and if x is 1 it becomes 0. We need to find a single arithmetic expression that does the same toggle.

keywords.explain: Switching a binary value from 0 to 1 or from 1 to 0.
keywords.example: If x = 0 after toggle x = 1; if x = 1 after toggle x = 0.

keywords.explain: Remainder after integer division. 1%x gives 1%1 = 0 and 1%0 is undefined (division by zero).

understand.visual_alt: A two-state toggle: state 0 and state 1 with arrows pointing to each other labeled 'toggle'.

given.aim: Find the arithmetic expression that produces the same result as the if-else toggle for x in {0, 1}.

terms.meaning: Conditional branching that changes x based on its current value.
terms.connects: This is the reference behavior we want to replicate.

given.plan: Substitute x = 0 and x = 1 into each option and check whether the result matches the toggle: 0->1 and 1->0.

steps.title: Check Option A: x = 1 + x
steps.apply:
When x = 0: 1 + 0 = 1. OK so far.
When x = 1: 1 + 1 = 2. Should be 0. FAIL.

steps.title: Check Option B: x = 1 - x
steps.apply:
When x = 0: 1 - 0 = 1. Correct (0 toggles to 1).
When x = 1: 1 - 1 = 0. Correct (1 toggles to 0).
Both cases match the if-else.
steps.note: Option B works perfectly for both values.

steps.title: Check Options C and D for completeness
steps.apply:
Option C: x = x - 1.
  x=0: 0-1 = -1 (not 0 or 1). FAIL.
Option D: x = 1%x.
  x=0: 1%0 is undefined (division by zero). FAIL.
steps.note: Neither C nor D is valid.

solution.result: B. x = 1 - x; correctly toggles x between 0 and 1.

questions.formula_note: Arithmetic complement of a binary variable: toggling x in {0,1} is achieved by x = 1 - x.

questions.question:
List the invariant assertions at points A, B, C, D and E in program given below:

Program division (input, output)
  Const dividend = 81; divisor = 9;
  Var remainder, quotient : integer
begin
  (* (dividend >= 0) AND (divisor > 0) *)
  remainder := dividend;
  quotient := 0;
  (*A*)
  While (remainder >= divisor) do
  begin
    (*B*)
    quotient := quotient + 1;
    remainder := remainder - divisor;
    (*C*)
  end
  (*D*)
end.

What is the loop invariant at point A (before the while loop starts)?
questions.answer: B. quotient * divisor + remainder = dividend AND remainder >= 0
questions.to_find: The invariant assertions at labeled program points in the integer division program.

understand.plain: The program computes quotient and remainder of dividend divided by divisor using repeated subtraction. A loop invariant is a condition that is always true at a specific program point, regardless of how many times the loop has executed. We need to identify what relationships hold between the variables at each labeled point.

keywords.explain: A predicate (logical condition) that holds true at a specific point in the loop for every iteration.
keywords.example: In a loop that adds 1 to sum n times, the invariant 'sum = number of iterations so far' holds at the start of each iteration.

keywords.explain: Finding quotient q and remainder r such that dividend = q * divisor + r with 0 <= r < divisor.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='500' height='240' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='38' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Integer Division by Repeated Subtraction</text><rect x='30' y='50' width='460' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Point A: quotient=0, remainder=dividend; q*div+rem=dividend AND rem>=0</text><rect x='30' y='90' width='460' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Point B (loop entry): q*div+rem=dividend AND rem>=divisor</text><rect x='30' y='130' width='460' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Point C (loop body end): q*div+rem=dividend AND rem>=0</text><rect x='30' y='170' width='460' height='30' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Point D (loop exit): q*div+rem=dividend AND 0<=rem<divisor</text><rect x='30' y='210' width='460' height='30' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='230' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Core invariant: quotient * divisor + remainder = dividend</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing invariant conditions at each labeled program point A through D in the division program.

given.aim: Identify the loop invariant at each program point that proves the division algorithm correct.

terms.meaning: The number being divided; constant throughout execution.
terms.connects: The core invariant equates quotient*divisor+remainder to this value.

terms.meaning: The number we divide by; constant throughout.
terms.connects: The loop condition checks remainder >= divisor; loop exit guarantees remainder < divisor.

terms.meaning: The fundamental relationship between quotient and remainder that must hold at all times.
terms.connects: This is the core invariant maintained throughout the loop.

given.plan: At each labeled point, determine which constraints on quotient, remainder and their relationship to dividend and divisor must hold. The core is always quotient*divisor + remainder = dividend; additional constraints change based on whether we are inside or outside the loop.

steps.title: Establish the core invariant
steps.apply:
Initially: quotient=0, remainder=dividend, so 0*divisor + dividend = dividend. True.
After each iteration: quotient increases by 1, remainder decreases by divisor, so quotient*divisor + remainder stays equal to dividend.
steps.note: This invariant is preserved by each iteration.

steps.title: Invariant at Point A (after initialization, before loop)
steps.apply:
quotient=0, remainder=dividend=81.
Invariant: quotient*divisor + remainder = dividend AND remainder >= 0.
Specifically: 0*9 + 81 = 81. remainder=81 >= 0.
steps.note: Point A: (quotient * divisor + remainder = dividend) AND (remainder >= 0).

steps.title: Invariant at Point B (start of loop body, condition true)
steps.apply:
The while condition (remainder >= divisor) is TRUE here.
Invariant: quotient*divisor + remainder = dividend AND remainder >= divisor.
steps.note: Point B adds the loop condition as an additional constraint.

steps.title: Invariant at Point C (end of loop body)
steps.apply:
quotient has been incremented by 1, remainder decreased by divisor.
Core: (quotient)*divisor + remainder = dividend, and remainder >= 0 (since we entered with remainder >= divisor and subtracted divisor).
steps.note: Point C: quotient*divisor + remainder = dividend AND remainder >= 0.

steps.title: Invariant at Point D (loop exit)
steps.apply:
The while condition is FALSE: remainder < divisor.
Combined: quotient*divisor + remainder = dividend AND 0 <= remainder < divisor.
This is exactly the postcondition of integer division.
steps.note: Point D proves correctness: result is a valid quotient and remainder.

solution.result: Core invariant: quotient * divisor + remainder = dividend. At A: remainder >= 0. At B: remainder >= divisor. At C: remainder >= 0. At D: 0 <= remainder < divisor.

questions.formula_note: Loop invariant for integer division by repeated subtraction: quotient * divisor + remainder = dividend, with the loop termination condition bounding the remainder.

questions.question:
Below figure is the flow-chart corresponding to a program to calculate the gcd of two integers, M and N respectively (M, N > 0). Use assertions at the cut points C1, C2 and C3 to prove that the flow-chart is correct.

The flow-chart implements the following logic:
  Read M, N
  x := M; y := N
  [C1]
  while x != y do
    if x > y then x := x - y
    else y := y - x
  [C2]
  write x (which equals gcd(M,N))
  [C3]

What is the invariant assertion at cut point C1 (just after initializing x=M, y=N)?
questions.answer: A. gcd(x, y) = gcd(M, N) AND x > 0 AND y > 0
questions.to_find: The invariant assertions at cut points C1, C2, C3 in the GCD flow-chart to prove its correctness.

understand.plain: The program computes GCD of two positive integers M and N using the subtraction-based Euclidean algorithm. A flow-chart is correct if assertions at each cut point capture what is true, and the final cut point implies the expected output. We need to find conditions that remain true at C1 (start of loop), C2 (inside loop after comparison), and C3 (after loop exits).

keywords.explain: The largest positive integer that divides both numbers without remainder.
keywords.example: gcd(12, 8) = 4 because 4 divides both 12 and 8, and no larger number does.

keywords.explain: gcd(x, y) = gcd(x-y, y) when x > y, and gcd(x, y) = gcd(x, y-x) when y > x. Loop until x = y.
keywords.example: gcd(12,8): 12>8 so x=4,y=8; then 4<8 so y=4,x=4; x==y so gcd=4.

keywords.explain: A logical predicate placed at a program point that must be true every time control reaches that point, used to prove program correctness.
keywords.example: At the start of a loop computing sum of n numbers: 'sum = sum of first i elements' is a cut point assertion.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='540' height='300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='520' height='280' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='270' y='35' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>GCD Flow-chart Assertions</text><rect x='30' y='50' width='480' height='35' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='73' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C1: gcd(x,y) = gcd(M,N)  AND  x > 0  AND  y > 0</text><rect x='30' y='95' width='480' height='35' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='270' y='118' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Loop: if x > y then x := x-y; else y := y-x</text><rect x='30' y='140' width='480' height='35' rx='4' class='bg'/><text x='270' y='163' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C2 (inside loop): gcd(x,y) = gcd(M,N) AND x > 0 AND y > 0 AND x != y</text><rect x='30' y='185' width='480' height='35' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='208' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Exit condition: x = y</text><rect x='30' y='230' width='480' height='35' rx='4' class='hi'/><text x='270' y='253' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C3: x = y = gcd(M,N)  (output is correct)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Flow-chart showing initialization x=M, y=N; then loop comparing x and y, subtracting the smaller from the larger, until x equals y; assertions C1, C2, C3 marked at key points.

given.aim: State invariant assertions at cut points C1, C2, C3 to formally prove the GCD flow-chart is correct.

terms.connects: x and y are initialized to M and N; gcd(x,y) must always equal gcd(M,N).

terms.meaning: Working variables that change during the loop; both stay positive throughout.
terms.example: Starting x=12, y=8; after one step x=4, y=8.
terms.connects: The invariant gcd(x,y)=gcd(M,N) holds because the subtraction step preserves the GCD.

terms.term: gcd(x-y, y) = gcd(x, y) when x > y
terms.meaning: The fundamental property of GCD that allows the subtraction form of the algorithm.
terms.connects: This property proves the invariant is maintained across loop iterations.

given.plan: Use the property gcd(x,y)=gcd(x-y,y) to show the GCD is preserved at each step. At C3, the loop exits with x=y, so x=y=gcd(M,N). State this chain of assertions to prove correctness.

steps.title: Assertion at C1 (after x:=M, y:=N; before entering loop)
steps.apply:
Initially x=M, y=N, so gcd(x,y)=gcd(M,N) trivially.
Also x>0 and y>0 since M,N>0.
C1: gcd(x,y) = gcd(M,N) AND x>0 AND y>0.
steps.note: This is the loop invariant that must be maintained.

steps.title: Prove the invariant is maintained by each iteration
steps.formula_raw: gcd(x-y, y) = gcd(x, y) when x > y
steps.apply:
Case x > y: new x' = x-y, y'=y.
  gcd(x',y') = gcd(x-y,y) = gcd(x,y) = gcd(M,N). Preserved.
  x' = x-y > 0 since x > y, and y'=y > 0.
Case y > x: symmetric argument.
So invariant holds after every iteration.
steps.note: The subtraction step preserves both the GCD value and positivity.

steps.title: Assertion at C2 (inside loop, condition x != y is true)
steps.apply:
C2: gcd(x,y)=gcd(M,N) AND x>0 AND y>0 AND x != y.
This is the invariant combined with the loop condition.
steps.note: C2 exists inside the loop body or at the loop decision point.

steps.title: Assertion at C3 (after loop exits, x = y)
steps.apply:
Loop exits when x = y.
From invariant: gcd(x,y) = gcd(M,N).
But gcd(x,x) = x, so x = gcd(M,N).
C3: x = y = gcd(M,N).
The output x is indeed the GCD. The program is correct.
steps.note: The post-condition matches the specification: output is gcd(M,N).

solution.result: C1: gcd(x,y)=gcd(M,N) AND x>0 AND y>0. C2: C1 AND x!=y. C3: x=y=gcd(M,N). These assertions prove the flow-chart correctly computes gcd(M,N).

questions.formula_note: Euclidean algorithm (subtraction form): gcd(x,y) = gcd(x-y,y) for x>y; loop invariant gcd(x,y)=gcd(M,N) is preserved; termination when x=y gives x=gcd(M,N).
